subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The entire chapter applies ratio and proportional reasoning to daily-life situations — cooking spice mixes, map scales, construction materials, paint mixing, cricket schedules, coin counts, food provisions, pumps, and workers. Every section (3.1–3.6) uses ratios or fractions in real-world problem contexts, making C-1.6 the organising spine of the chapter.

Section 3.6 architecturally builds the student's ability to discover two distinct numerical patterns — the constant-quotient pattern of direct proportions and the constant-product pattern of inverse proportions (xy = k). The speed-time table (5×18 = 15×6 = 30×3 = 60×1.5 = 90) exemplifies pattern discovery in numbers, and the chapter systematically contrasts both rules across 11 worked examples and 14 exercises.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 3.5 provides a full pie chart construction sequence — reducing a ratio using HCF, dividing 360° proportionally, constructing sectors with protractor, and labelling. Exercises E-6, E-7, and E-8 extend this to three further data sets, making graphical data representation a substantive strand of the chapter.

Constructing geometric shapes with specified properties is engaged in section 3.5 (drawing a circle and marking successive angles to build a pie chart) and in WE-5 and E-4 (constructing triangles with angles or side lengths in a given ratio using compass and straightedge).

Section 3.6's inverse proportion word problems (WE-8 through WE-11, E-16–E-22) all require setting up and solving a linear equation for an unknown value using x₁y₁ = x₂y₂, providing repeated practice in posing and solving one-variable equations in real-world contexts.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to apply ratio and proportional reasoning — multi-term ratios, division of a whole in a ratio, and inverse proportions — to solve daily-life problems, by developing fluency with the direct proportionality relationship (constant quotient) and the inverse proportionality relationship (constant product).
